paragraphs.text: AFM

The Dutch Authority for Financial Markets (AFM) likes to add additional information regarding the consultation on the Digital Fairness Act. In the annex you will find a copy of the original questionnaire with our answers marked in yellow. The AFM strongly supports the Commission's objectives regarding the DFA. We see examples in our supervisory practice that have also emerged based on the Fitness Check carried out by the Commission. This includes: consumers are exposed to practices such as deceptive or manipulative interface design (dark patterns), addictive features, unfair personalisation practices that exploit consumers' vulnerabilities, misleading marketing by online (f) influencers, as well as difficulties in managing digital contracts.

In this written contribution we briefly outline what our mandate is and how the DFA has an impact on it (paragraph 1). Thereafter, we will provide further input on some key topics of the future DFA (paragraph 2). We conclude with some overall comments and references to our own research and online documentation that may provide Inspiration for drafting the new legislation.
In addition, we hope that this response will ultimately lead to more discussion about the new DFA. We hope to be able to contribute to this at a later stage as well.
Mandate AFM

The Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM) has been responsible for supervising the operation of the financial markets since 1 March 2002. This means that AFM supervises the conduct of the entire financial market sector: Savings, investment, insurance, loans, pensions, capital markets, asset management, accountancy and financial reporting. The AFM is committed to promoting fair and transparent financial markets. As an independent market conduct authority, we contribute to sustainable financial well-being in the Netherlands. The AFM currently shares a responsibility with the Authority for Consumers and Markets (ACM) for two of the three Directives that will be affected by the DFA. This group: Directive 2005/29/EC on unfair commercial practices and Directive 2011/83/EU on consumer rights. The AFM has a responsibility in this regard as far as financial services are concerned.
Additional input on key topics of the future DFA
Dark patterns

It would be useful to clarify dark patterns and even ban obvious manipulative and misleading patterns. On the other hand, it is important that we recognise that context is important for assessing online choice architecture – it can be beneficial, as well as as harmful, and can be used Positively. Please also refer to principle 5 of the CERRE paper. If legislation prescribes too strict what is and is not allowed in terms of design, there is a risk that positive management is also discouraged. Not every form of influence is necessarily harmful. This also depends on:
the purpose (e.g. informed vs. manipulate).
the context in which it is applied
the target group (e.g. children vs adults).
the degree of transparency and control the user has.
Giving consumers more control

We believe consumers should always be given the option to disable personalisation. Consumers must have easy and accessible control over whether or not they are because a personalised/algorithm-driven environment online. This also means that a non-personalised environment must always remain available. The same thing plays out in the recent lawsuit against Meta: Judge: Instagram and Facebook must give people an algorithm-free option.
Digital interventions to consider:
Time awareness tools
Usage limits: let users set time limits themselves (like with TikTok or Instagram).
Time notifications: provide feedback on time spent (“You have been active for 45 minutes”).
Break design
Stopping moments in interfaces: design natural pauses (e.g. after 10 scrolls a “do you want to continue?” question).
No autoplay as standard: let users choose whether they are because content to play automatically.
Transparency about algorithms
What do I see this? Use online click buttons on recommendations with additional information.
Customizable feed: Latvian users choose between Chronological or algorithmic.
Opt-out of addictive features
Allow users to disable addictive elements such as streaks, notifications or gamification.
Protective defaults for young people
Youth accounts may receive fewer notifications, no autoplay, and a limit on nighttime use by default.
Addressing problematic personalisation practices

We believe a similar approach here comes in Handy as with the choice environment: depending on the purpose for which companies use personalisation, this can pose a risk. At the same time, it also offers opportunities for guidance towards sensible (financial) behavior. There is also an additional problem, i.e. that with extensive personalisation everyone can in fact be ‘made vulnerable’. See, for example, this study by Helberger and colleagues. Finally, also a relevant discussion here: how do you gain insight into problematic personalisation when the online choice environment looks different for each individual? Principle 9 of this document may also be useful here: CERRE paper.
Preventing harmful practices by (f) influencers

A basic measure that can be tasks is to introduce a disclosure obligation with regard to commercial communications and sponsorship. Sponsorship notifications/disclosures for influencers could help, but would benefit from further research. Currently, there has been little research into what and how such a
notification can best be made. These disclosures do appear to contribute to the ability to presumnise advertising, but the question is to what extent they can actually influence behavior.

A further step – at the same time more controversial – is to hold platforms accountable for ensuring that influencer content is properly labelled (as harmful/unreliable). This could then affect the algorithm Positively in showing less harmful content.
Proportionality

If the AFM answered ‘yes, additional regulations are needed’ to almost every question in the consultation, we are also aware of the importance of burden reduction for companies. Hopefully, proportionality can be achieved in the future DFA, possibly also by using a risk-based approach.
Coherence with other regulatory frameworks

Commission publications regarding the DFA mention the Coincidence with recent legislative initiatives such as DSA regarding “dark patterns” and the AI Act regarding “prohibited use of AI”. To avoid difficult interpretation issues and double burdens for businesses, we would like to ask the Commission to further clarify any possible Confluence with existing legislative initiatives and to prevent overlap or contradictions. In addition to the possible link with the AI Act and the DSA, there is a possibility that there is also overlap with the DMFSD and Accessibility Directive.

The DMFSD (Directive on distance marketing of financial services) is a European directive that harmonises the rules for the distance selling of financial services to consumers in order to improve consumer protection. The directive contains, among other things, rules against Deception by “dark patterns” and requires a simple online option to terminate agreements.

The Accessibility Directive refers to the European Accessibility Act (EAA), and mandates that a wide range of digital and physical products and services must be accessible to people with disabilities across the EU. This also includes rules about the provision of information that cannot simply be done digitally (there is a question about this in the consultation).
Focus on the best interest of the customer

Separately from the DFA and the targeted existing consumer legislation the AFM has a broader mandate which emanates from financial regulatory frameworks. An important pillar of financial services legislation is the duty of care towards the customer and the obligation for financial market participants to demonstrate that they have made a balanced assessment of different interests and that the product is suitable for what the customer wants or needs. The latter includes obligations concerning thorough procedures in which the aforementioned is an element and requires clear descriptions on who the target group is for certain products and how the product or services fits the needs of the customer.
The studies below related to the topics of the future DFA. Our publications may provide Inspiration for the development of the DFA.
AFM Presents points of attention for personalisation of premiums and conditions

Based on standards in the Product Approval and Review Process (PARP), developers of financial products must take into account in a balanced manner the interests of consumers. A financial market participant must be able to demonstrate the result of the weighing of interests. Where the PARP standard is not sufficient, the AFM can rely on the General Duty of Care.
https://www.afm.nl/nl-nl/sector/actueel/2021/juni/aandachtspunten-gepersonaliseerde-beprijzing
**Finfluencing**

By way of introduction: the AFM has no role with influencers in general, but as soon as an influencer says or does something with regard to financial services, our mandate may be affected and financial services legislation may apply. We have communicated the following about this:
https://www.afm.nl/en/sector/themas/digitalisering/finfluencing.
**Deepdive on hyperpersonalisation: to be published in November.**
Annex
We were not able to access the online questionnaire, that's why a copy of the file is included in this annex, with our answers marked in yellow.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Public consultation for the Digital Fairness Act impact Assessment

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: As a result of the development of EU consumer law over the last 50 years, EU consumers are among the most protected in the world, online and offline. EU consumer law aims at ensuring a high level of protection for European consumers and at contributing to the better functioning of the EU single market and the competitiveness of businesses. It protects consumers against unfair commercial practices, giving them the right to withdraw from contracts and be properly informed before purchasing goods or services, while harmonising such rules and providing a level-playing field for traders operating cross-border in the EU. With the rise of e-commerce, the development of new technologies and increasing use of AI, it is important to ensure that EU consumer law continues to provide a high level of consumer protection in the current digital environment.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: In recent years, the EU has significantly reinforced its digital rulebook, with the Digital Services Act ('DSA'), the Digital Markets Act ('DMA') and the Artificial Intelligence Act ('AIA'), in addition to the Data Act and the Audiovisual Media Services Directive ('AVMSD'), among others. These instruments complement EU consumer protection laws and reduce the risks and harms associated with specific problems online. In particular, the DSA introduced new restrictions to several unfair practices occurring on online platforms. The Commission has actively exercised its enforcement powers to ensure compliance and issued guidelines, such as on the protection of minors online. All in all, however, the application of consumer protection rules in the digital area in conjunction with other digital legislation, which provided rules on certain types of traders (e.g. online platforms) or technologies (e.g. AI systems), is complex and specific gaps remain.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: In this context, in October 2024, the Commission published a “Digital Fairness Fitness Check” which evaluated three EU consumer law Directives, analysing whether the existing EU consumer protection legislation is still relevant, effective, efficient, in the view of the new digital challenges. In particular, consumers are too often exposed to practices such as deceptive or manipulative interface design (dark patterns), addictive features, unfair personalisation practices that exploit consumers’ vulnerabilities, misleading marketing by online influencers, as well as difficulties in managing digital contracts. The Fitness Check concluded the estimated financial detriment suffered by consumers as a result of the identified problems is at least EUR 7.9 billion per year. The Fitness Check also underlined the current lack of clarity and legal certainty regarding unfair commercial practices, pointing at the lack of enforcement, the existing regulatory gaps and market fragmentation. These problems have a negative impact on the Single Market and the level playing field for EU businesses, who face unfair competition from non-compliant competitors. Moreover, the exponential growth of ecommerce, in particular with non-EU traders, has

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: raised many pressing challenges across different policy areas, including product safety and unfair marketing practices.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The aim of this public consultation is to gather citizens' and stakeholders' views on potential improvements in EU consumer law to strengthen the protection of consumers in general – and of minors as consumers in particular – in the digital environment and ensuring a level-playing field for traders.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: This public consultation will be open for 12 weeks and respondents can reply in any EU official language. The results of all consultation activities, including this public consultation, will inform the preparation of a possible Digital Fairness Act.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: It will take you approx. 20 minutes to fill in the questionnaire. We recommend that you regularly save a draft of the questionnaire as you fill it in and submit the questionnaire ("Submit" button at the very end) before the end of the consultation period. You can download the questionnaire in PDF format to help you prepare or discuss the reply within your organisation and can download an electronic copy of your reply.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: For the multiple-choice questions below, some of the answers are not Combinable. If you wish to change your answer, please unselect your first answer by clicking on it again, then click on the answer (s) that you wish to select.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Question: I'M replying as/on behalf of a:

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: **National consumer protection authority**

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Netherlands Authority for Financial Markets

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Dark patterns are unfair commercial practices deployed through the design of digital interfaces that can influence consumers to take decisions they would not have tasks otherwise. Examples of such practices may include but are not limited to: presenting choices in a leading manner (e.g. Trader's preferred choice in colour, prominently displayed, other option(s) in black and white and difficult to find), using countdown timers to create urgency or asking misleading questions using double negatives.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Question: Concerning dark patterns, do you think any new EU actions should be tasks to improve the protection of consumers and the functioning of the Single Market? (Multiple answers possible.)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: No actions are needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, non-regulatory measures are needed (e.g. guidance)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, more effective enforcement by public authorities of existing rules is needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, new binding rules are needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: No opinion/Don't know

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Addictive design features in digital products are those that make consumers spend more time and money online than intended, e.g. infinite scrolling (where a page loads content with no distinct end), content that disappears quickly (Ephemeral stories), autoplay (that allows video or audio files to play without user's intervention), applying penalties for disengagement (such as breaking a streak) or recommender systems that are steered to increase the consumer's engagement.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Question: Concerning addictive design, do you think any new EU actions should be tasks to improve the protection of consumers and the functioning of the Single Market? (Multiple answers possible.)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: No actions are needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, non-regulatory measures are needed (e.g. guidance)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, more effective enforcement by public authorities of existing rules is needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, new binding rules are needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: No opinion/Don't know

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 3 – Specific features in digital products, such as in video games
paragraphs.text: Specific concerns having arisen with regard to certain features in digital products, for example in-app purchases that include uncertainty-based rewards, imitating gambling (e.g. loot boxes), pay-to-progress and pay-to-win mechanisms, and in-app purchases offered in exchange for virtual currencies that blur the real world value of those transactions.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 3 – Specific features in digital products, such as in video games
paragraphs.text: Question: Concerning specific features in digital products, such as video games, do you think that any new EU actions should be tasks to improve the protection of consumers and the harmonisation in the Single Market? (Multiple answers possible)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 3 – Specific features in digital products, such as in video games
paragraphs.text: Answer: No actions are needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 3 – Specific features in digital products, such as in video games
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, non-regulatory measures are needed (e.g. guidance)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 3 – Specific features in digital products, such as in video games
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, more effective enforcement by public authorities of existing rules is needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 3 – Specific features in digital products, such as in video games
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, new binding rules are needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 3 – Specific features in digital products, such as in video games
paragraphs.text: Answer: No opinion/Don't know

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Consumers often find personalised offers and content useful (e.g. personalised advertising). At the same time, many consumers are concerned about how their personal data are used to Personalise commercial offers, and many have the impression that information about their vulnerabilities (e.g. personal problems, financial challenges, or negative mental states) is used unfairly for commercial purposes.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Question: Concerning unfair personalisation practices, do you think that any new EU actions should be tasks to improve the protection and the functioning of the Single Market? (Multiple answers possible)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: No actions are needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, non-regulatory measures are needed (e.g. guidance)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, more effective enforcement by public authorities of existing rules is needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, new binding rules are needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: No opinion/Don't know

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: With the increasing importance of social media for consumer transactions, reports of problematic commercial practices have become more prominent. Groups arise with regard to social media influencers, for example as regards hidden marketing and the promotion and sale of potentially harmful products.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: Question: Concerning unfair influencer marketing, do you think that any new EU actions should be tasks to improve the protection of consumers and the functioning of the Single Market? (Multiple answers possible)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: Answer: No actions are needed

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, non-regulatory measures are needed (e.g. guidance)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 5 – Harmful practices by social media influencers
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes, more effective enforcement by public authorities of existing rules is needed Answer: Yes, new binding rules are needed Answer: No opinion/Don't know

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 6 – Unfair marketing related to pricing
paragraphs.text: Consumers may face unfair practices related to the marketing of the price, such as drip pricing (failing to disclose upfront mandatory and unavoidable costs and fees and adding them later in the course of the booking), advertising attractive 'starting' prices while automatically applying dynamic price increases (rendering such starting prices unrealistic for a majority of buyers) and misleading practices regarding price comparisons based on vague reference prices that give a false impression of reduction of the selling price.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 6 – Unfair marketing related to pricing
paragraphs.text: Question: Concerning unfair marketing related to pricing, do you think that any new EU actions should be tasks to improve the protection of consumers and the functioning of the Single Market? (Multiple answers possible)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Section 6 – Unfair marketing related to pricing
paragraphs.text: Answer: No actions are needed Answer: Yes, non-regulatory measures are needed (e.g. guidance) Answer: Yes, more effective enforcement by public authorities of existing rules is needed Answer: Yes, new binding rules are needed Answer: No opinion/Don't know

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Consumers may face issues with digital contracts, notably regarding the cancellation or renewal of Subscriptions, or the conversion of free trials into paid Subscriptions, and with automated contracts. In addition, consumers may not have the possibility to contact a person when trying to reach the customer service about their contract.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Question: Considering issues with digital contracts, do you think that any new EU actions should be tasks to improve the protection of consumers and the functioning of the Single Market? (Multiple answers possible)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: No actions are needed Answer: Yes, non-regulatory measures are needed (e.g. guidance) Answer: Yes, more effective enforcement by public authorities of existing rules is needed Answer: Yes, new binding rules are needed Answer: No opinion/Don't know

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Any possible legislative change proposed in any of the areas above should contribute to enhanced consumer protection and simplification of the regulatory environment. In addition, the Digital Fairness Act could also address other issues with a view to further reducing compliance costs while improving effective consumer protection. The Digital Fairness Fitness Check has identified potential for targeted simplification and burden reduction for traders, specifically in the area of information requirements and the right of withdrawal.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Question: In your view, are there any concrete measures to simplify consumer laws that could reduce the burden for businesses while maintaining the same level of consumer protection?

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes Answer: No Answer: No opinion/Don't know

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: *Question: Do you think certain types of information should be provided to consumers solely in digital form?

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Yes. Please describe concretely which information

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: No Please explain: In many cases it will be fine for consumers to receive information digitally, but it is important that with impactful products there is also the option to receive information physically. This is relevant in the context of the accessibility of services, also for people who are less skilled online.

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: No opinion/Don't know

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: *Question: In your view, in which of the following areas would EU actions reduce single market fragmentation that may currently exist due to diverging national laws or interpretations by national courts or authorities? (Multiple answers possible)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Online interface design (dark patterns, such as presenting choices in a leading manner)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Addictive design (features that make consumers spend more time and money online than intended, e.g. infinite scrolling)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Features in certain digital products, such as video games (e.g. loot boxes or pay-to-progress or pay-to-win mechanisms)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Personalised commercial practices (such as advertising and pricing based on personal data/profiling)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Commercial practices by social media influencers (e.g. lack of disclosure of the commercial intent, harmful claims about certain products or services)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Pricing practices (e.g. adding unavoidable fees during the ordering process which make the final price differing from the headline price initially advertised)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Answer: Digital contracts (e.g. difficult exercise of the right to cancel Subscriptions)

section_path: Public consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Question: Do you have specific suggestions, requests for clarification or concerns with regard to the interaction of cross-cutting EU consumer protection legislation with other existing EU legislation, including the Audiovisual Media Services Directive, the Digital Services Act, the Digital Markets Act, the Artificial Intelligence Act, or the EU Digital Identity Framework? Please be specific and provide evidence to support your views.

Question: In addition to the above, do you think that any further EU actions should be tasks to improve the protection of consumers and the functioning of the Single Market in the digital environment in a more general way? (Multiple answers possible.)
Answer: No actions are needed
Answer: Yes, non-regulatory measures are needed (e.g. guidance)
Answer: Yes, more effective enforcement by public authorities of existing rules is needed
Answer: Yes, new binding rules are needed
Answer: No opinion/Don't know
Question: Do you have further suggestions for improving consumer protection and enforcement in the digital Sphere and contributing to a level playing field for traders in the EU?
Do you have specific suggestions concerning the protection of minors?
Question: Would you like to submit documents? Please upload your file (s) here.
